text:
🎧 YouGlish
When arriving at a restaurant, say: {{c1::"Hi, table for two please." or "Do you have anything available?"}}
explanation:
Pronunciation: 'table for two' → /ˈteɪ·bəl·fər·tuː/
• 'for' → /fər/ (schwa — extremely common reduction of 'for' in speech)
• 'two' → /tuː/ (stays stressed — it's content, not function)

Common host responses:
• 'Right this way.' — follow them
• 'It'll be about 20 minutes.' → /ɪ·ɾl·bi·ə·baʊ·ˈtwɛn·i·ˈmɪ·nɪts/ — Flap T in 'It'll'!
• 'Do you have a reservation?' → /duː·juh·hæ·və·rɛz·ər·ˈveɪ·ʃən/ — schwa everywhere
usage:
• "Table for two, please — we don't have a reservation."
  • "Is there a wait?" → /ɪz·ðɛr·ə·weɪt/
  • "Could we sit outside if possible?"
  • "Can we get a booth?" → /kə·nwi·gɛ·tuh·buːð/ — linking: 'get a' → 'ge-tuh'
  • "We're actually three now — can you add a chair?"
spanish: 'Table for two please.' → 'teɪ·bəl·fər·tuː·pliːz' — 'for' se reduce a /fər/ (schwa), muy común en inglés coloquial. 'It'll be about 20 minutes' → /ɪ·ɾl·bi·ə·baʊ·twɛ·ni·mɪ·nɪts/ — Flap T en 'it'll'.

scenario: 🍽️ Restaurant — Reading the menu and asking questions
text:
🎧 YouGlish
The most useful question to a server is: {{c1::"What do you recommend?" or "What's your most popular dish?"}}
explanation:
Native pronunciation:
• 'What do you recommend?' → /wʌ·djuh·rɛ·kə·mɛnd/
• 'What do you' → yod coalescence: 'do you' → /djuh/
• 'recommend' → /rɛ·kə·ˈmɛnd/ — stress on last syllable, schwa in middle

Menu vocabulary:
• Entrée = main dish (US), not starter like in French
• Appetizer = starter / first course
• Side = side dish (fries, salad, etc.)
• A la carte = ordering items separately
• Prix fixe = set price menu (often 2-3 courses)
usage:
• "What's the special today?" → /wʌ·sðuh·ˈspɛ·ʃəl·tuh·deɪ/
  • "Is this spicy?" (always ask before ordering Thai/Indian)
  • "Does this come with a side?"
  • "Can I substitute the fries for a salad?" → /kə·naɪ·ˈsʌb·stɪ·tjuːt/
  • "What does 'house dressing' mean?" → the restaurant's own recipe dressing
spanish: 'What do you recommend?' → /wʌ·djuh·rɛ·kə·mɛnd/ — Yod coalescence: 'do you' → /djuh/. Vocabulario: Entrée = plato principal (en EE.UU., NO es entrante como en francés). Appetizer = entrante. Side = guarnición. Prix fixe = menú de precio fijo.

text:
🎧 YouGlish
When ordering food, say: {{c1::"I'll have the [dish], please."}} — NOT 'I want the...' or 'Give me the...'
explanation:
'I'll have the...' → /aɪl·hæv·ðuh.../
• 'I'll' = I will (contracted) — very smooth /aɪl/
• 'have the' → linking: 'have' ends in /v/, 'the' starts with /ð/ → /hæv·ðuh/

🧠 'I want the...' is grammatically fine but sounds demanding. 'Give me the...' without 'please' is rude in American English.

Specifying doneness:
• Rare → red center / Blue → basically raw
• Medium rare → pink center (most popular for steak)
• Medium → slightly pink
• Well done → fully cooked through
usage:
• "I'll have the salmon, please." /aɪl·hæv·ðuh·ˈsæm·ən·pliːz/
  • "And I'll have the burger, medium."
  • "Can I get the pasta without the onions?"
  • "I'll start with the Caesar salad."
  • "We'll share the appetizer." /wiːl·ʃɛr·ðuh·æ·pɪ·taɪ·zər/
spanish: 'I'll have the...' → /aɪl·hæv·ðuh.../ — la forma más cortés y natural de pedir comida. 'I want' es un poco exigente. 'Give me' sin 'please' es grosero en inglés americano. Para el punto de la carne: rare (rojo), medium rare (rosado — el más popular), medium (ligeramente rosado), well done (completamente cocido).

scenario: 🍽️ Restaurant — During the meal: server check-ins
text:
🎧 YouGlish
The server will ask: "How is everything?" or "How's everything tasting?" The natural reply is: {{c1::"Everything's great, thank you!" or "It's delicious — compliments to the chef."}}
explanation:
Server question pronunciation:
• 'How's everything?' → /haʊz·ˈɛv·ri·θɪŋ/
• 'everything' → /ˈɛv·ri·θɪŋ/ — schwa absorbed, rapid middle syllable

If something is wrong:
• 'Actually, this is a bit under-seasoned.' (diplomatic)
• 'I'm sorry, but could I get this heated up a bit more?' (food came cold)
• 'Excuse me, I think this might not be what I ordered.'

🧠 Americans rarely complain directly — they hedge with 'actually', 'I think', 'a bit'.
usage:
• If great: "Everything's wonderful, thanks!"
  • If okay: "Yeah, it's pretty good!" (honest but positive)
  • If food is wrong: "Actually, I ordered the salmon, not the tuna."
  • If service is slow: "Whenever you get a chance, could we get some more water?"
  • Best compliment: "Compliments to the chef — this is incredible."
spanish: Cuando el mesero pregunta '¿Cómo está todo?', la respuesta natural es 'Everything's great, thank you!' o 'It's delicious.' Si algo está mal, los americanos son indirectos: 'Actually, I think this might be...' — nunca acusan directamente. 'Compliments to the chef' = felicidades al cocinero.

text:
🎧 YouGlish
To get the bill in the US say: {{c1::"Can we get the check, please?"}} — NOT 'bill' (that's British English).
explanation:
Pronunciation: 'Can we get the check?' → /kən·wi·gɛt·ðuh·tʃɛk/
• 'Can we' → /kən·wi/ (schwa: 'can' → /kən/)
• 'get the' → /gɛt·ðuh/ (schwa: 'the' → /ðuh/)

Splitting the bill:
• 'Can we split this?' → each person pays half
• 'Can we get separate checks?' → each person pays their own
• 'I've got this one.' → you're treating (paying for the whole table)
• 'Let me get this.' → same, I'm paying

US tipping at sit-down restaurant: 18-20% minimum (standard), 25% for great service.
usage:
• "Can we get the check, whenever you have a second?"
  • "We'd like to split it three ways, please."
  • "Can I get a receipt for that?" → /fər·ðæt/ (schwa on 'for')
  • "Keep the change." (cash)
  • "This one's on me." = I'm paying for everyone
spanish: En EE.UU. di 'the check' (no 'the bill' — eso es inglés británico). 'Can we get the check?' → /kən·wi·gɛt·ðuh·tʃɛk/ — 'can' y 'the' se reducen a schwa. Propina en restaurante con servicio de mesa: 18-20% mínimo. 'I've got this one' = yo invito (pago yo todo).
